entries.title: On Thin Ice by Charlie Walker review – a Siberian adventure goes off the rails
entries.summary: Moments of beauty and horror punctuate this account of an ill-timed sub-zero camping trip In late February 2022, Charlie Walker set off on a hiking holiday in northern Siberia. In temperatures as low as -50C, he intended to trek a thousand miles north along the frozen surface of the river Lena, then out across the solid Laptev Sea to the port town of Tiksi, before finally flying home to London. “T
entries.content:
In late February 2022, Charlie Walker set off on a hiking holiday in northern Siberia. In temperatures as low as -50C, he intended to trek a thousand miles north along the frozen surface of the river Lena, then out across the solid Laptev Sea to the port town of Tiksi, before finally flying home to London. “The plan,” he writes, “was simple in concept.” A sub-zero camping trip through a region populated by wolves and bears and scarred by centuries of historical trauma, in a country that was about to invade one of its neighbours: what could possibly go wrong?

On Thin Ice is Walker’s third travelogue. Previous excursions have included a 43,000-mile bicycle ride across three continents, a 1,000-mile solo walk through the Gobi desert, and a river trip down the Congo in a leaky canoe. When not performing incredible feats of forward motion, according to his website, he gives talks to business leaders on subjects such as “risk taking” and “the line between courage and recklessness”. Reading his latest, it’s clear he’s familiar with the terrain on both sides of that line.

Landing in the Siberian city of Yakutsk with his skis, sled, tent and a “comically huge sleeping bag” Walker is busy provisioning when the full-scale invasion of Ukraine begins. With the Foreign Office advising against travel to Russia, he briefly considers abandoning his plans. But, reasoning that “Yakutsk is comfortably closer to the Alaskan city of Anchorage than it is to Kyiv”, he presses on.

“The journey would be different now,” he writes. “As one of the last Western tourists in Russia, I was presented with a unique opportunity … to record the thoughts and feelings of ordinary Russian people, neither soldiers nor politicians. Living far from the front lines, their various opinions would provide insight into the silent majority of 144 million citizens. This seemed worth the risk of remaining inside a police state where already-limited freedoms of expression could evaporate overnight.”

Armed with a flare gun, a can of pepper spray and a dagger for protection from the wolves and bears, Walker spends weeks trudging along ice roads – zimniki – by day, shivering in his tent at night, and occasionally experiencing the extremes of Siberian hospitality. The lorry drivers he meets on the zimniki stop for photos, sharing coffee and conversation. One scolds him for pitching his tent on the road: many truckers fuel themselves with vodka, while some watch television as they drive. He’s treated to local delicacies: grilled horse ribs, reindeer shin pie, a sausage made from aged horse blood (“the puck of boiled blood escaped its horse-gut housing and smeared across the roof of my mouth, setting fast like some ungodly grouting”). Indigenous Sakha women place a wolf’s penis amulet around his neck and offer pancakes to Bayani, “spirit lord of the taiga”, to safeguard his passage.

Many of the people he encounters are more than willing to talk about Ukraine. “Ukraine’s a corrupt country run by Nazis,” declares one young Sakha man. “Ukraine belongs to Russia,” says another. Even in areas so barren trees struggle to grow, Kremlin propaganda is deeply rooted. “I heard that President Putin is saving the Ukrainian people and I’m happy to know they’re being helped,” a lone Eveni herder tells Walker over a meal of roasted reindeer spine. Almost everyone seems to believe some version of the same story: that Putin saved Russia from the chaos of the 90s, and whatever he does is for the best. One wonders – a million Russian casualties later – how that story has weathered.

More than votive pancakes, more than daggers and flares, it soon becomes apparent that the survival tool Walker really should have packed was the business card of a competent local lawyer. It’s not bears who stalk him across the ice, but the FSB, who take an increasingly hostile interest in this inexplicable Englishman, especially as his intended destination, Tiksi, turns out to be a military base. He is accused of “committing journalism”; of being a spy; people he encounters are harassed, others rush to inform on him. He takes insane risks, recording his own “anti-Putin rants” on a Dictaphone – when anti-war journalists face 15-year sentences – and signing a confession without having it translated first. He ends up in a detention centre, where he spends an agonising month awaiting deportation. Reflecting on his choices, he remembers an old Russian saying: “You saw what you were buying, so eat up, even if you choke on it.”

At the start of his journey, Walker had hoped he might see a rosier side of Siberia. There are moments of beauty – the aurora borealis, rare bursts of warmth from the locals – but almost everywhere else is horror and corruption. Growing sinkholes, decaying towns, roads packed with the skeletons of the prisoners who died building them, a “blackened wasteland” the size of Belgium left by wildfires. Walker evokes it all with a masterly touch. Nobody, after reading this book, will be in a rush to book tickets to Siberia, least of all its author, who is now banned from the country.

entries.title: Venus, Vanishing by Rebecca Birrell review – art, desire and the rise of the Third Reich
entries.summary: This portrait of a Jewish painter in bohemian Berlin is filled with insightful observationsIn the summer of 1937, two exhibitions sponsored by the Nazis opened in Munich. For the Degenerate Art exhibition, hundreds of confiscated works by banned modern artists – including Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, Pablo Picasso – were gathered: a derisive demonstration of the supposedly anti-German spirit of c
entries.content:
In the summer of 1937, two exhibitions sponsored by the Nazis opened in Munich. For the Degenerate Art exhibition, hundreds of confiscated works by banned modern artists – including Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, Pablo Picasso – were gathered: a derisive demonstration of the supposedly anti-German spirit of contemporary culture. The show was also meant as a counterpoint to the first Great German Art Exhibition, essentially an art fair for state-sanctioned work, which ran annually until 1944. The official style of Aryan art was traditionalist neoclassicism; popular subjects included idealised female nudes, pastoral landscapes and heroic battle scenes. Hitler was among the main buyers. You probably haven’t heard of any of those artists.

What if one of them were secretly a Jew, working under a pseudonym? Rebecca Birrell’s debut novel imagines this scenario, telling the story of a fictional Jewish artist who comes of age in Berlin in the last days of the Weimar republic. Hannah Sherman is working as a seamstress for the family business when she learns that she is about to be married off. She runs away, finding lodgings using the “desirable Christian” name of Schmidt, and begins to realise her artistic ambitions. A free-thinking New Woman, she also takes two lovers: Saul, a museum curator, and Charlotte, a dancer at a lesbian bar where Hannah likes to sketch.

We all know where history is going. The worsening economic and political situation – which Hannah, our narrator, prefers to allude to only obliquely – engulfs what might otherwise have been a straightforward story of an artist’s progress. As the Great Depression sets in, Hannah turns to a former client for mending work. Elke Grese, a glamorous older woman, sees Hannah’s art and makes an offer of patronage so generous it would be mad to refuse. But as Hannah soon learns, there are strings attached. How far will she go to survive?

Birrell is an art historian by training; in 2021 she published This Dark Country, an idiosyncratic blend of group biography and cultural criticism about female artists working with still life in early 20th-century England. Her deep understanding of artists and their work is evident here. Both Hannah’s inner thoughts and her conversations with others are filled with imaginative and insightful observations, including but not limited to the old master paintings in Saul’s workplace (the Kaiser Friedrich, a real museum whose holdings were destroyed in a fire in 1945) and the postcard reproductions of famous works that she trades back and forth with family, friends and lovers.

While painting her mother, for instance, Hannah is prompted to reflect on Rembrandt’s series of portraits of an elderly woman believed to be his own mother: “In my favourite, his mother’s ageing skin resembles wax, the wrinkles etched into her flesh echoed in the detail of her embroidered cloak, life weighing so heavily upon her she can hardly keep her eyes open.”

Hannah sees the rest of the world through the eyes of an artist, too. On a car journey: “From the windows of the taxi, the clouds were zinc white, paint fresh from its tube.” This makes her an arresting narrator when it comes to visual description – although perhaps a touch too ready with similes and metaphors: are leaves really “like small hands in the breeze”?

By contrast, her emotional landscape is not as vividly rendered. Without giving away too much of the plot – there are some good twisty turns – it often isn’t clear why Hannah gets involved with certain other characters, who seem more like sketches than people. When talking about feelings, she tends to resort to abstract nouns: “fear stirred in my chest”, “shock gave way to shame” and so on. You get the sense that she doesn’t understand her own motivations. “I didn’t interrogate why I wanted a role,” she admits at one point, when describing her choice to insert herself as a background figure in a painting of someone else. This is not unrealistic: she is, after all, a painter, not a novelist. But it can make for frustrating reading.

Towards the end of the book, Birrell offers a glimpse of her own narratorial range with an abrupt shift in perspective, and for the first time enters the mind of another character. Elke – until now a rather distant and mysterious figure – is sitting in a car on a street in Munich, about to pay a visit to the Great German Art Exhibition of 1942. It’s a welcome moment of strangeness closer to the quirky style of Birrell’s nonfiction: a reminder that literature, like art, is at its best when embracing its fullest capacity for invention.

entries.title: Cool Machine by Colson Whitehead review – final part of the Harlem Trilogy is freewheeling fun
entries.summary: The grit and grandeur of 80s New York drive this rollicking story of an underground wheeler-dealerIn the summer of 1977 a bolt of lightning struck a substation on the Hudson River, knocking out power across New York City. Two nights of darkness sparked a wave of looting as Harlem and Bronx teenagers swiped high-end items from the downtown stores. They took speaker systems and turntables to experim
entries.content:
In the summer of 1977 a bolt of lightning struck a substation on the Hudson River, knocking out power across New York City. Two nights of darkness sparked a wave of looting as Harlem and Bronx teenagers swiped high-end items from the downtown stores. They took speaker systems and turntables to experiment with at home. They taught themselves how to scratch and mix, and sang self-styled scat lyrics into stolen microphones. Hip-hop, according to urban legend, was the child of a lightning strike and a robbing spree.

It’s art from crime, thinks Ray Carney, the hardscrabble hero of Colson Whitehead’s rollicking Cool Machine, the final part of his Harlem Trilogy. Or possibly vice versa, given that the man dislikes rap music and nurses a sneaking affection for old-school smash-and-grab. For years Carney has led the sort of intrepid double life that would make Spider-Man proud, supplementing his furniture business on 125th Street with a lucrative sideline as an underworld fence. In his stealthy, dogged fashion, he helps facilitate a process he describes as “the churn”: the movement of stolen goods through New York. The churn fuels the economy and helps refresh the culture. It’s the city’s equivalent of an endocrine system.

The Harlem Trilogy, like its hero, is a cool-headed trickster, more ambitious and far-reaching than its dimestore facade would suggest. Carney’s tale can be relished as a set of shaggy-dog crime capers that bound from the high-stakes heist to the mysterious bag of loot to the fraught getaway through the rat-infested back alley. But it is also – perhaps principally – a story of New York through the decades, and its semi-autonomous circles of black and white society. Harlem Shuffle, the first book in the series, hotwired the action to the 60s civil rights movement. The second, 2023’s Crook Manifesto, played out during the 70s “Fear City” era, when the infrastructure collapsed, the murder rate spiked and Carney’s store doubled as a blaxploitation film set.

Now along comes Cool Machine, the grandstanding final chapter, which pitches us into the dirty, regenerative 1980s. That means hip-hop on the stereo, graffiti on the subway cars and a widening gulf between rich and poor. It means, in this case, a plot that plays like pop-art Marxist noir, jam-packed with cultural references and thrumming with class and racial tensions.

Searching for a secret route into the five-star Waldorf Astoria, Carney encounters a Morlock-style community in the train sheds beneath Manhattan, a subterranean tent city governed by the self-styled “Mayor Crotch”. The homeless people in the tunnels, Carney realises, are casualties of the prosperity above. “The richer those up top got, the more miserable and numerous those below became. And the more cast-offs and have-nots, the higher you have to build to get away from them.”

Carney is a creature of the underworld too, but he’s also a middleman or a bridge, directing the traffic up and down to the point where the colour line becomes porous. Skipping through three interlinked tales from 1981 to 1986, Cool Machine uncovers the seeds of 21st-century cultural appropriation in the trade of African artefacts and sports memorabilia. White and black crooks squabble over the ownership of a Jesse Owens Olympic medal. A Ngil mask from Gabon electrifies the grungy East Village arts scene. A decade ago, Carney’s director friend Zippo was shooting blaxploitation pictures uptown. Now he’s hanging with the arthouse crowd, working on a redubbed version of Alfred Hitchcock’s 1963 classic in which the N-word is substituted for every mention of birds. Hitchcock’s original film was always a parable for racial desegregation, Zippo insists. So he’s simply taking the subtext and making it text.

Whitehead initially envisioned the Harlem Trilogy as a jaunty entertainment, a break from the serious Pulitzer-winning business of The Underground Railroad and The Nickel Boys. Cool Machine specifically covers the New York of his youth, a scene he experienced first-hand, which only adds to the air of vivid, freewheeling fun. Whitehead maps the streets, labels the landmarks and dips in at intervals with handy background information. He takes a dark and dangerous corner of history and makes it feel illuminated, magnified, even borderline family-friendly.

It’s the churn, paradoxically, that keeps the tale honest. It adds grit and grandeur to the sugar hit of nostalgia. The churn, Carney decides, applies to more than the flow of stolen goods. It is the great whip and roil of the city itself; the successive waves of immigrants washing through the individual neighbourhoods. “The enclaves and ghettoes rose and fell and were reborn. That was the churn, too – the underlying melody of death and rebirth in blocks and avenues. You wake up to discover the city has unveiled another version when you weren’t looking.” Carney is now in his 50s and has one eye on the exit door. He’s toiling to maintain his position, fighting the currents of a city that is as ageless and elemental as the Mississippi River. New York, he comes to realise, doesn’t care whether he lives or dies. It’s all-seeing and indifferent and just keeps rolling along.

entries.title: These Wild English by Nicola Wilding review – explaining a mother’s drift to the far right
entries.summary: A documentary-maker probes family history and the darker corners of the internet to solve an urgent political puzzleWhen Sandra Forsyth began marching in support of the English Defence League, her daughter Nicola Wilding hoped it was a phase. The Cumbria-bred, Dover-dwelling Sandra was about to turn 60 and had been dealing with a lot of stress. Not only had her son been sentenced to two years in p
entries.content:
When Sandra Forsyth began marching in support of the English Defence League, her daughter Nicola Wilding hoped it was a phase. The Cumbria-bred, Dover-dwelling Sandra was about to turn 60 and had been dealing with a lot of stress. Not only had her son been sentenced to two years in prison for armed robbery, but she was working at a home for adults with complex needs. She liked the job, but it could be gruelling – especially when clients hurled furniture at her or ripped sinks off walls.

Then, in May 2013, came the death of Lee Rigby, a drummer with the Second Battalion of the Royal Regiment of Fusiliers who was murdered by two Islamist terrorists using machetes. Sandra didn’t know Rigby, Wilding notes, but his death nonetheless brought her pent-up rage to the surface. She began watching videos on Facebook by a man named Tommy Robinson, who wanted to know why the country’s brave soldiers were being murdered. By September of that year, she was protesting on the streets of London.

Inspired by the puzzle of her mother’s late-life activism, These Wild English is a heartfelt and evocative memoir of Sandra who, up until her death in 2024, endured poverty, upheaval and abusive partners, as well as a heart condition leading to two rounds of surgery. “How did my mum, this proud tough woman who had brought up four kids pretty much alone, end up marching alongside far-right extremists chanting ‘No more refugees’?” Wilding asks. “And how did my brother – a kind, smart, funny man who loved his kids – how did he end up in prison? … Was it us? Did we do it to ourselves? Or was it something else – something bigger than us?”

The “us” in this story is important. Wilding, the eldest of Forsyth’s children and one of the few in her family to find stability through education and a career, is a TV documentary maker specialising in poverty and rural working-class communities, though here she is no dispassionate observer. She also has an eye on the bigger picture, noting how wider events and the social and economic decisions taken in Westminster can affect the lives of vulnerable individuals and communities. For the Forsyth family, these have included the winter of discontent, mass unemployment and Thatcher’s right-to-buy scheme in the 1970s and 80s; the foot-and-mouth outbreak and the financial crisis in the 2000s; and the Tory austerity programme, Brexit referendum and resurgence of the far right in the 2010s.

Wilding isn’t the first author to examine the alienation of the British working classes and the ways they have been exploited, pilloried and misunderstood: Kerry Hudson’s Lowborn, Joel Budd’s Underdogs and Rebecca Smith’s Rural have all offered their own perspectives. In our social media-fanned, post-Brexit discourse, the white working classes are often cast as aggressors waging vicious war on immigrant communities, having been manipulated by self-interested politicians deliberately stoking division. Wilding has little time for such one-dimensional depictions, despite her clear dismay at her mother’s flirtation with the far right.

Instead, writing in thoughtful and elegantly understated prose, she digs into the roots of Sandra’s dissatisfaction, telling a story that spans three generations, starting out with her early life in the 1960s on her father’s farm in Eden Valley, Cumbria, where she was tasked with looking after her siblings during the recurring periods when her own mother did a runner. Exasperated by the drudgery of parenting and rural living, Nana Forsyth would leave the family for weeks on end before returning home clutching an olive branch in the form of sweets for the kids and cigarettes for her husband.

At 18, Sandra became pregnant and attempted to make a life with the baby’s father, moving to a cottage up the road from the farm. But within a couple of years, he had left and Sandra was a penniless single mother. And so began a peripatetic pattern that would continue throughout her life: a new man, a new home, an implosion followed by deeper debt and the quest for a fresh start.

In the book’s acknowledgments, Wilding credits the writer and farmer James Rebanks, a former neighbour in Cumbria, for “seeing this book before I could”. It seems Wilding took some convincing to turn her documentarian’s gaze on her own flesh and blood, though, happily, she did. Full of grit and compassion, These Wild English blends the personal and political to illustrate the ways families are shaped by circumstance and inherited trauma, and can be buffeted by forces beyond their control. At its heart is Sandra, a protagonist for the ages who is wayward, charismatic, fierce, caring and – wisely or not – always up for a fight.

entries.title: A Sudden Flicker of Light by David Thomson review – a brilliantly eccentric history of the movies
entries.summary: From the golden age of Hollywood to harder times today, this is a rapturous account of more than a century of cinemaFilm critic, historian and author of more than 40 books, David Thomson is back with another three-hour free jazz solo on the subject of the movies, though it’s a performance delivered with the magnificent elan of a 1940s big band leader. He’s as brilliant, eccentric, exasperating and
entries.content:
Film critic, historian and author of more than 40 books, David Thomson is back with another three-hour free jazz solo on the subject of the movies, though it’s a performance delivered with the magnificent elan of a 1940s big band leader. He’s as brilliant, eccentric, exasperating and absorbing as ever.

By “the movies”, incidentally, he means Hollywood, with a side order of established European and east Asian arthouse: he takes us from the cine-prehistory of photographer Eadweard Muybridge to Fritz Lang’s Metropolis, DW Griffith’s Birth of a Nation with its sinister Ku Klux Klan hero-worship, Chaplin, Stroheim, Hitchcock, Capra, Renoir, Bogart, Marilyn Monroe in The Seven-Year Itch, Monroe Stahr in The Last Tycoon, Scorsese’s GoodFellas, David Lynch’s Mulholland Drive (“Is it drive or dream?”), Kirk Douglas and his alleged rape of Natalie Wood, Barbara Loden’s Wanda, Sean Baker’s Anora, Tracey Emin’s Why I Never Became a Dancer and much more. He subtitles his book a “revisionist” history.

Revisionist? Well, it certainly takes us to wintry and sombre ponderings on the movies’ (perennial) tendency to coarsen and to cheapen, to enforce cultural mob-rule. He cites King Vidor’s 1928 film The Crowd, starring James Murray, a former extra plucked from the ranks and groomed for glory but whose awful destiny was to descend into alcoholism and homelessness – back into the crowd. He emphasises movies’ amazing populist-democratic power and reach, their power embodied by the fact that, unlike in the theatre or concert hall, a film’s action could not be stopped or affected by the crowd in front of it. Like a theologian dragged by doubts to the foot of the cross, Thomson wonders if the movies will always lack literature’s way of allowing us to enlarge our imaginations, as opposed to forcing a submission to the screen.

He descants on the triumph of Stephen Graham’s Netflix drama Adolescence and uses it to suggest that the big-screen theatrical experience may indeed be on the way out, because “the image on our home screens is brighter and more transporting. If you are inclined to dispute that, look very closely.” Well, I have looked closely and I do dispute it. All the same, Thomson’s contrarian, non-handwringing opinion on the subject is refreshing. Only he could have got away with that gleeful heresy, designed to wake us up to the commercial forces that brought big screens into being in the first place.

So it is revisionist. In a way. But the book’s body language is non-revisionist: it’s a straightforwardly rapturous, rhapsodic celebration of the extraordinary adventure of cinema in the 20th century and beyond with an old-fashioned feel. The author is a great believer in the importance of profits and grosses, in telling us how much money the key films made, as if that proves anything, though he’s also a great respecter of the importance of Sight and Sound magazine’s decennial Greatest Film of All Time poll.

The point is that Thomson, with that marvellous ambition that is all his own, always wants to recount the whole story, to start again with that task, to encompass the giant shape in its awe-inspiring entirety, the “whole equation”, to borrow F Scott Fitzgerald’s phrase which he brilliantly used as the title for a previous book. That is his revisionism: always to take on the whole thing. He rockets through his story while firing off into the slipstream provocative generalisations and opinions, gags and barbs.

He contrasts the darker moods of the American New Wave with the contemporary sunlit positivity of Steven Spielberg, who, he says, is “the most decisive figure in the history of film, a guy who came upon an art experimenting with difficulty or doubt and talked it in off the ledge”. He says that the film industry’s pious and glib dedication to the shopworn concept of “love” has brought it to an evolutionary crisis: “The species is at a point where LOVE can start to feel as hollow as HOLLYWOOD above Los Angeles.”

But weirdly, Thomson has a priestly tendency to intone the names of the accepted greats and to expect a kind of cinephile genuflexion in each case. Clive James did something like it in his TV documentary series Fame in the 20th Century: he would give an introductory upward-lift of acclaim to the first mention of each new famous name, as if bringing them on to a chatshow, and there is something of that in Thomson’s prose which can stimulate, amuse and bewilder all at once.

He takes his title from a moment in Henry James’s 1881 novel The Portrait of a Lady, which he regards a classic proto-cinematic revelation: heroine Isabel Archer has married Gilbert Osmond and one day comes home to find their mutual friend Madame Merle and Osmond in an attitude of disconcerting intimacy and ease, he seated and she standing: “… the thing made an image, lasting only a moment, like a sudden flicker of light”. Thomson enthuses about that moment, whose vividness hints that James has mastered the arts of cinematographer, director and editor, and capriciously adds: “I want to bring this scene to life, with maybe Meryl Streep as Madame Merle, and who would you like as Osmond?” (It is a characteristically wilful and faintly baffling aside: Thomson appears here to disregard the excellent film version with John Malkovich as Osmond and Barbara Hershey as Madame Merle, directed by Jane Campion and starring Nicole Kidman, whose superfan biography Thomson has written. To be fair, Campion’s film doesn’t actually include that flickering moment.)

So how about now? Are the movies beginning to flicker in another sense, flicker out into darkness? Well, the gloomy-elegaic insistence on the end of cinema is a tiresome kind of cinephile affectation that’s been around for decades, usually from writers who are in fact mourning the passing of their own movie-filled youth and middle age. Thomson thankfully does not indulge it. But there are flickers of anxiety whose implications the author doesn’t explore at any length.

In the midst of discussing Sergei Eisenstein, whose longing to make films was shared by so many, Thomson remarks: “That longing lasted until … well, the end of the century? But I think it’s in retreat now. Do we feel we’ve seen it all?” That throwaway lands in our lap. In retreat? Seen it all? If he is serious, shouldn’t the author stop what he’s doing and pursue the point more rigorously?

Well, it could be that this book is not a conventional critical work at all, nothing so workaday; it is rather quasi-fictional, like an action painting made from melted celluloid in which Thomson is the creator-hero, splashing around on the canvas in his multicoloured liquid element. Or simply an epic novel: he alludes repeatedly to EL Doctorow’s 1975 book Ragtime, about immigration and the American dream in the first years of the 20th century, that mixes real figures with imaginary ones. Here, Thomson is both the narrator and underdog, meeting with legends in the crowded streets of Los Angeles or New York, feeling their pain, sensing their euphoria, foretelling their downfall or triumph. I very much hope that this is not his last book. I can’t imagine film criticism or films without him.

entries.title: Yellow Pine by Claire Vaye Watkins review – messy portrait of protest subculture
entries.summary: Watkins should have been the ideal writer for this sympathetic take on new age spirituality and desert eco-activism – but the results are confusingRose, the protagonist of Claire Vaye Watkins’s latest novel, has retreated from the world to live in Nothingness Flats, a cinder-block cabin in the Nevada desert. On indefinite leave from her job at an environmental organisation, Rose works on her Al-An
entries.content:
Rose, the protagonist of Claire Vaye Watkins’s latest novel, has retreated from the world to live in Nothingness Flats, a cinder-block cabin in the Nevada desert. On indefinite leave from her job at an environmental organisation, Rose works on her Al-Anon recovery, goes hiking and joins protests against the Yellow Pine of the title: a solar array being built over a fragile desert ecosystem. She writes in her journal: “I am on this side of the portal. I have compassion in my heart for anyone back on the other side, sending emails, bickering, buying useless shit, getting run by the algorithm, living in the past.” As the story begins, she is reunited with her first love, Miles, who has “fallen apart beautifully: bopped in and out of law school, then spent six years in what’s left of the redwoods reading horny 400-year-old poems before bailing on his PhD”. Soon, Rose realises what she truly wants, this side of the portal, is to have his child.

You don’t often see a sympathetic take on the subculture of 21st-century hippies, with their eclectic leftwing philosophies, new age spirituality and earnest striving to do good and be kind. It’s an important strain in modern life, and one typically dismissed with facile mockery and condescension. As a serious treatment of it, Yellow Pine has notable strengths. The love story between Rose and Miles is very real and specific to this world, and gives a backbone of plot to material that otherwise tends to sprawl. Watkins’s prose can be musical and keenly attentive, and she excels at conveying starry-eyed love. “[Miles] was brilliant, peaceful, grounded as a mountain, kind even when he was blue, an unabashed loner agog at the whole burning world.”

However, the plot meanders and has odd discrepancies. Crucially, in the beginning, Miles agrees to have unprotected sex with Rose, knowing she will keep the baby if she gets pregnant. Later, confusingly, the main conflict between them is whether he’s ready for a child, with no mention of their previous conversation.

It also becomes maddeningly unclear how Watkins wants us to view her characters. The narration treats them affectionately – we’re clearly meant to be on their side – but their actions are often jarringly self-centred. Rose, for instance, engineers a fully paid leave from her job by threatening to support staff efforts to unionise, but she never considers really doing it; it’s just a means for her to be paid for doing nothing. She also narcs out all the local weed farms because she worries their fertiliser is leaching into her water. Many pages later, we learn that when these farms are raided by police, all their undocumented workers are deported. Rose never pauses to contemplate her role in their eventual fate. She has chosen to leave her young daughter, Juniper, in the care of her father in Pennsylvania, and Rose often laments how painful the separation is to them both. But she never considers moving closer to Juniper, and this history forms no part of her thinking about having another baby.

It’s also a problem that there are so many scenes where the characters lecture each other. This is reasonable when we’re being educated about why industrial solar arrays are a legitimate target for protest, information we need to understand the plot. It’s not so excusable when characters are making speeches about prehistoric goddess worship, the gnostic gospels or the decline in the quality of commercially available apples. In one particularly baffling scene, Rose brings a stack of climate books with her to a bar and recites long passages from them to a nonplussed bartender. Open Yellow Pine at random and you’re liable to find someone saying: “since we’re all made of star stuff and the big wheel has been turning for eternity, it makes sense that some people feel like part of you … Like maybe your potassium and my potassium were once together in the bones of some fish in Lake Lahontan”, or, “Some scholars do surmise that marriage was patriarchal religion’s incentive to power via state-sanctioned violence against women in an alliance intended to suppress the resistant Goddess worshippers”.

This often feels like hostile satire, and there are occasional judgmental speech tags such as “Rose brayed” and “Joskie squealed”. But this is apparently not the intention; in her prefatory letter to the reader, Watkins describes Yellow Pine not as satire, but as “a spiritually seeking eco-socialist novel about disillusionment and re-enchantment, and the daily practice of defending the sacred”. Watkins’s history as a political writer couldn’t be stronger: the viral essay On Pandering, the climate classic Gold Fame Citrus. She should have been the ideal person to write a sympathetic novel about this world. But in Yellow Pine, what could have been an important addition to the genre of climate fiction is instead an often likable but ultimately confusing mess.

entries.title: Tell us: what have you been reading this month?
entries.summary: We would like to hear about the books you’ve particularly enjoyed this monthAs part of The Guardian’s “what we’re reading” series, we would like to hear about the books you’ve particularly enjoyed this month.Have you read a book in recent weeks – fiction or non-fiction – that you’d recommend? Tell us all about it below. Continue reading...
entries.content:
As part of The Guardian’s “what we’re reading” series, we would like to hear about the books you’ve particularly enjoyed this month.

Have you read a book in recent weeks – fiction or non-fiction – that you’d recommend? Tell us all about it below.

Share your recommendations

You can get in touch by filling in the form below. Your responses are secure as the form is encrypted and only the Guardian has access to your contributions.

One of our journalists will be in contact before we publish, so please do leave contact details.

If you’re having trouble using the form, click here. Read terms of service here and privacy policy here.

entries.title: The best books to read in July: new paperbacks from Andrew O’Hagan, Miriam Toews and Oyinkan Braithwaite
entries.summary: Looking for a new reading recommendation? Here are some fantastic new paperbacks, from the gripping story of an international murder case to a state-of-the-nation yarn*** Continue reading...
entries.content:
Fiction

Fiction

Cursed Daughters

Oyinkan Braithwaite

Repeat a family story often enough, and it becomes a kind of legend – or a curse. The Faloduns at the centre of Cursed Daughters share tales of heartbroken women across the generations who just can’t seem to hold on to a man. There’s Fikayo, whose husband left after he tired of tending to her chronic illness; Afoke, who seduced her younger sister’s boyfriend; Feranmi, the matriarch of the family, who got pregnant by a married man and received the curse from the man’s first wife.

Nigerian-British novelist Oyinkan Braithwaite splashed on to the literary scene in 2018 with My Sister, the Serial Killer, a taut debut about sisterhood, jealousy and murder. Cursed Daughters, her second novel, swaps true crime for a more atmospheric spookiness, but it shares a lingering fascination with the dark secrets that might bind the women of a family together. The Falodun curse forms an ominous, ever-hovering presence for the three main characters – Monife, Ebun and Eniiyi – as they grow up, fall in love, and attempt to defy the supernatural forces that seem to hold their family in thrall. Chelsea Leu

£8.99 (RRP £9.99) – purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Fiction

Pan

Michael Clune

The narrator of American nonfiction author Michael Clune’s first novel is the 15-year-old Nicholas, who lives with his father in a housing development so cheap and deracinated it inspires existential terror. Just as frightening is Nicholas’s sense that “anything can come in”. One day in January, what comes into Nicholas is the god Pan – a possessing, deranging, life-threatening spirit. Or that, anyhow, is how Nicholas comes to interpret his increasingly disabling anxiety.

Pan is remarkable for the honesty of its treatment of both mental illness and adolescence. It shows more successfully than any other book I’ve read how these can be experienced as black magic – indeed, it allows that they might be black magic.

Nicholas becomes convinced that he is perpetually at risk of leaving his body – specifically, that his “looking/thinking could pour or leap out” of his head – and his friends, also being 15 years old, are ready to believe it, too. They are easy prey for Ian, a college-age man who sets himself up as a small-time cult leader among these high-school kids. Soon the group is staging rituals incorporating sex, drugs and animal sacrifice.

A reader who approaches Pan expecting the usual rewards of a coming-of-age story will be sorely disappointed. It offers not answers but visions; not growth but lambent revelation; not closure but openings. Nicholas ends his inner journey without arriving at the cure he has been pursuing. But when we close the book, we find ourselves in a larger world. Sandra Newman

£8.99 (RRP £9.99) – purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Fiction

Among Friends

Hal Ebbott

Amos and Emerson are the best of friends; everyone knows this. They met on the first day of college and bonded immediately despite their surface differences (Emerson is rich and handsome, Amos poor but clever). They are a model of male intimacy and understanding: confiding in each other, trusting each other, hugging each other (“real, loving hugs, clutches without irony”). Theirs is truly a friendship for the ages.

Or so it seems. For on the weekend of Emerson’s 52nd birthday, an occasion at the centre of Hal Ebbott’s probing and insightful debut novel, something happens that changes everything – and raises the question of whether we can ever truly know anyone.

Among Friends is a bracingly honest and affectingly intimate depiction of abuse, family dynamics and self-deceit. It is sharply observed and psychologically astute, somehow both passionate and dispassionate, and it upends its characters’ lives so ruthlessly and revealingly that it is hard not to take pleasure in a false facade being finally smashed. Christopher Shrimpton

£8.49 (RRP £9.99) – purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Fiction

The Matchbox Girl

Alice Jolly

We meet our fierce narrator, Adelheid Brunner, when she is brought into a children’s hospital by her grandmother, who cannot cope with the little girl’s fixations. Adelheid is obsessed with the matchboxes of the title, which she is constantly studying, ordering and occasionally discarding. In the hospital, she finds that she and her fellow child inmates are the object of obsessive study in turn by their doctors – sometimes understood, sometimes valued, and then, tragically, sometimes discarded.

Adelheid sees how certain disabilities spark particular interest from one of the key doctors, Dr A, who is intrigued by the children he calls his “little professors”. This, we come to understand, is Dr Hans Asperger, whose research in the Vienna children’s hospital in the 1930s laid the groundwork for the understanding of autism.

Adelheid works out how she needs to present herself in order to thrive in this milieu: to show that she is valuable and not to be discarded. “One can put on the coat of a Life,” she realises, “and perhaps change for another garment when the need arises.” She is able to leave the hospital for a time, and watch the rise of nazism from her position as a waitress in her grandmother’s crowded cafe, returning as a ward assistant during the dark years of the second world war.

In Naomi Klein’s book Doppelganger, she also explores the way that the Vienna children’s hospital under Asperger became a “key node in the system of sorting who would live and who would be murdered”. Klein dissects Asperger’s shift from care to callousness, from curiosity to murder, in order to ask how we can resist that shift right now. As a novelist, Jolly is less interested in the bigger lessons and more interested in the moment-by-moment anguish and chaos of those times. But there is a synergy between Klein’s work and Jolly’s, both of whom seem to be on a journey to identify what makes us human and what destroys that humanity. Natasha Walter

£8.99 (RRP £9.99) – purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Fiction

Female, Nude

Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett

It is the summer of 2019, and Sophie Evans, the reckless protagonist of Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett’s unsettling second novel, has arrived on an idyllic island in the Cyclades with her university friends Helena, Iris and Alessia to celebrate Helena’s forthcoming marriage.

In the 10 years that have passed since they first met as students, the differences between the women have become more pronounced: money has “made itself known”. Elegant, chilly Iris, whose parents have bought her a place in Peckham, works in publishing; the family of spoilt, patrician art dealer Alessia seem practically to own the island on which the women are holidaying; and Helena’s aspiration is to be a trophy wife with a house full of “nice things”.

By contrast, Sophie – whose father is an electrician and mother the full-time carer for her disabled sister – is working in a museum shop while she tries to make her way as an artist. She is also under pressure from her reliable, thoughtful partner, Greg, to have a baby, when what she urgently wants is freedom to paint. Apparently understanding this, Alessia commissions a nude portrait from Sophie, to be painted during their time on the island in her private studio. But when the beautiful Ky, waiter, archaeologist and extraordinary lover, appears at the villa and begins to look at Sophie in a certain way, the rivalries that have been simmering turn toxic, and unease becomes something more dangerous.

Female, Nude is an energetic and ambitious novel. Cosslett – a Guardian columnist – is excellent at the sensual detail of light and food and physical pleasure; she immediately engages us with a seductive drama of friendship between women in an exotic and glamorous White Lotus-like location, while at the same time offering a serious-minded interrogation of art. Christobel Kent

£9.89 (RRP £10.99) – purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Fiction

Everything Will Swallow You

Tom Cox

This shambling but intricate yarn of friendship, loyalty, alienation and record collecting features a depressed nature writer called Billy Stackpole. He is sitting around his hand-forged firebowl when he utters the woeful/hopeful plea: “This sounds weird but I’ve never had a big sloppy cardigan and I wish I did … Just something you can throw on, at a time like this. Maybe in a nice earthy green, a bit mossy.”

As far as Billy knows, he’s speaking to a group of eight human beings. However, also listening in, incognito, is the novel’s cosiest and most unusual character: a long-nosed, sleek-haired magical sea creature with 24 fingers who is capable of passing for a large brown dog, but also of hoovering, gardening, reading Barbara Kingsolver novels, speaking six languages, giving wise life advice, and most excellent knitting. Meet Carl – who, because he’s nice, will secretly knit Billy a cardigan.

Carl is one half of a charming odd couple along with Liverpudlian record dealer Eric. Everything Will Swallow You is the story of Eric’s life, without and then with his supernatural companion. But it’s also a materially hopeful “state of England” novel. Our social fabric may be fraying, but we’re still warm and woolly, most of the time. Toby Litt

£8.99 (RRP £9.99) – purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Fiction

Havoc

Rebecca Wait

Even if it wasn’t perched on a cliff on the south coast, the position of St Anne’s, Eastbourne – the decaying girls’ school that is the setting for Rebecca Wait’s gleefully macabre novel, Havoc – might reasonably be described as precarious. Deeply eccentric, staffed by the barely employable, and permanently teetering on the brink of bankruptcy, St Anne’s hangs on, against all the odds. And then, in 1984, Ida Campbell turns up on the doorstep, in possession of a full scholarship and rather a lot of baggage.

Sixteen years old and already an outcast, Ida is in flight from her hapless mother, her foul-tempered sister, the small community in the Western Isles to which they have been transplanted, and the nameless scandal that has ruined their lives. St Anne’s is to be Ida’s salvation, but it soon dawns on her that the school might not be quite the refuge she had hoped for.

In Havoc, Waits mines the rich seam of girls’ school fiction to delirious and rewarding effect. There are welcome echoes of St Trinian’s, and there is abundant Ealing comedy in the madcap chases through school corridors and machinations in the lighting gallery during the school play. Yet beneath the comedy lies a distinctly unsettling undertone: the girls experience a convincingly visceral terror that edges towards Shirley Jackson territory and gives their hysteria an extra dimension. This, along with a genuine unexpectedness in the characterisation and a lot of very funny dialogue, loosens things up and brings real originality to the game. Combined with excellent pacing, a plot so deliciously thick you could stand a spoon up in it, and the boldness required to splice a darker thread into the narrative, it all adds up to a thoroughly satisfying contribution to a happily capacious genre. Christobel Kent

£9.89 (RRP £10.99) – purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Thriller

Thriller

The Persian

David McCloskey

Former CIA analyst McCloskey’s fourth novel centres on Jewish Iranian dentist Kam Esfahani. Dissatisfied with life in Sweden, where his family relocated when driven out of Iran, and wanting the wherewithal to move to California, he accepts an offer from the chief of Mossad’s Caesarea Division. Returning to Tehran, he runs a fake dental practice as cover for assisting in “sowing chaos and mayhem in Iran”. Things go awry when he enlists double agent Roya Shabani, widow of an Iranian scientist killed by the Israelis. The book takes the form of a series of confessions that Kam, now caught and imprisoned, is forced to write by his torturer, and these documents – which may or may not reveal the whole truth – are interspersed with flashbacks. Kam’s cynical tone and mordant humour serve to underline not only the horror, but also the inherent hypocrisy of the endless cycle of violence and retribution: this masterly novel is tragically topical and utterly gripping. Laura Wilson

£8.49 (RRP £9.99) - purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Essays

Essays

On Friendship

Andrew O’Hagan

Novelist Andrew O’Hagan’s new book comprises eight brief essays reworked from a series recorded last year for Radio 4. The mode is reminiscence: we hear about a lost childhood friend from the council estate where he grew up in 1970s North Ayrshire; about former colleagues at the London Review of Books, where O’Hagan made his name in the 1990s; about his adult daughter’s bygone imaginary friend. He considers why actors, politicians and Republicans make bad friends, why the novelist Colm Tóibín makes such a good one, and how the experience of friendship is shaped by bereavement and the internet.

The most intriguing item here concerns the late Irish novelist Edna O’Brien, whom he first met in London in 2009 after leaving Seamus Heaney’s 70th birthday party. When he invites her to lunch at the Wolseley in Mayfair (“Perfect … ask for the corner table, Lucian Freud’s table”), it’s the beginning of a 15-year friendship during which “we called upon each other to complete thoughts we were unable to have alone”, in O’Hagan’s curious phrase, glancingly elaborated on when he later recalls “the soft sonatas we used to listen to while I helped Edna with her manuscripts”.

Of the various names dropped in these pages, she’s the only one permitted an insight into O’Hagan himself. In most of his anecdotes, he’s the guy who comes out best – whether as a schoolboy weeping over Charlotte’s Web when cruder classmates laughed, or as a hardy reveller who is nevertheless earliest to rise the morning after a big night out – so your ears prick up a little when, apropos of nothing, O’Brien tells O’Hagan (observed, for once, rather than observing) that she can see he’s “a wounded man who handles it very impeccably and very plausibly”.

For a memoir – which is what On Friendship is, however piecemeal – that would seem to leave money on the table, to say the least. Ultimately, these memories and musings – in appealingly slimline hardback, eminently gift-ready – leave you wondering about the larger autobiography O’Hagan might write, if and when he chooses. Anthony Cummins

£8.49 (RRP £9.99) - purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Memoir

Memoir

A Truce That Is Not Peace

Miriam Toews

Asking himself “Why I write”, George Orwell gave four reasons: aesthetic enthusiasm, historical impulse, political purpose and sheer egoism. Asked the same question ahead of a literary conference in Mexico City, Miriam Toews mentions the teenage letters she sent from Europe to her sister Marjorie (Marj or M as she calls her) as the reason she became a writer. Sorry, that won’t do for an answer, she’s told. Try again.

This book is a triumph – a meditation on writing, suicide, guilt and silence; a fragmented account of Toews’s life so far; and an illustration of why she’s one of Canada’s most admired writers. Though the Mexico City organiser missed the point, those letters to her sister were indeed her prime reason for writing. “Why do I write? Because she asked me to.” That was the deal they made. “You live. And I’ll write.”

Marj suffered from depression and in 2010 she killed herself, 12 years after their father Melvin had done the same. “You people (meaning, my family) don’t talk about your pain,” Toews’s partner tells her, “you just kill yourselves.” Toews herself came close to doing so one day while walking by the river Assiniboine, in Winnipeg, but rather than throwing herself in the water she threw her mobile phone instead.

Despite the sadness and depression, there’s a lot of laughter in the family, including an episode when they take a boat out to an uninhabited island, disembark for a picnic and return to find the boat has disappeared – rather than wail they all begin to laugh (later, miraculously, the boat drifts back to shore). Toews’s mother, a Scrabble genius, remains indomitably cheerful despite her losses, and is arguably the heroine of the book.

Why were her father and sister so silent, Toews asks her mother. “That’s easy, she replies, it was something they could control.” For Toews, writing serves the same purpose, absorbing her sorrow and rage.

“Are you OK? I mean, you know, OK? I miss you so much,” Toews writes to her sister. “I wish I could take your sadness away.” She’s still trying, all these years later, closing with a 2023 letter to Marj (“You’ll like this”) about a stranger arriving at a Christmas Eve family gathering and being made welcome, though it turns out he has not been invited. There’s Toews for you. Her work’s so intimate you worry you’re intruding, but it’s fine, she welcomes you in. Blake Morrison

£9.34 (RRP £10.99) - purchase at the Guardian bookshop

True crime

True crime

Story of a Murder

Hallie Rubenhold

On the evening of 31 January 1910, two couples dined together at a house in Hilldrop Crescent, on the borders of Holloway, London. The hosts, Dr Crippen and his wife, Belle Elmore, had been entertaining their friends, Clara and Paul Martinetti, until the small hours. After some difficulty fetching a cab, the visitors headed home around 1.30am. It was the last time they, or anyone else, would see Elmore alive. When her colleagues at the Music Hall Ladies’ Guild made inquiries about their friend – she was treasurer of the organisation – Crippen told them she had gone off to America to deal with a family crisis. Some weeks later they were informed she had died of double pneumonia in Los Angeles.

Thus was sparked an international murder case, one of the most notorious in Britain, later called “the crime of the century”, of which Hallie Rubenhold gives an engrossing account. Her energy as a researcher is indefatigable, and admirable. It helped to burnish her award-winning previous book The Five, which ingeniously flipped the mythology of Jack the Ripper to focus upon the lives of his victims. She means to repeat the trick here by shifting the centre of gravity from the murderer to the women his shadow crossed, but it doesn’t quite come off. Unlike the Ripper, we know the killer’s identity, and Crippen, for all his mild-mannered blankness, remains the fiendish engine of the story. The mystery here is not who, but why, and the book cannot supply a convincing answer. Time and again, Rubenhold has to admit to the limitations of life-writing when her research meets a dead end: “is unknown”…“is a mystery”… “There is no indication of how” …“is uncertain”…“will never be known”.

Not that Story of a Murder: The Wives, the Mistress and Doctor Crippen wants for excitement or intrigue. The discovery of a dismembered corpse beneath the cellar floor at Hilldrop Crescent was the first of two sensational turning points in the tale, the bodily remains bearing traces of hyoscine, a poison that Crippen had recently purchased, alongside a scrap of his own pyjamas. The ineptitude was almost as remarkable as the callousness. Anthony Quinn

£9.34 (RRP £10.99) - purchase at the Guardian bookshop

Biography

Biography

Gwyneth

Amy Odell

Gwyneth: The Biography opens, where else, with the vaginal egg, an episode that has come to stand for Paltrow’s general ability to sell dumb ideas to credulous rich women using widespread mockery as her marketing rocket fuel. (In case you need a reminder: this was the $66 jade egg Paltrow sold via her lifestyle brand Goop that promised various health benefits upon insertion.) Amy Odell’s book, billed as delivering “insight and behind-the-scenes details of Paltrow’s relationships, family, friendships, iconic films”, as well as her creation of Goop, takes no particular stand on this, nor on many of Paltrow’s more divisive episodes, instead offering us what feels like an earnest jog back through the actor and wellness guru’s years of fame.

I ripped through Odell’s account of Paltrow’s youth as the simultaneously indulged and benignly neglected daughter of two showbusiness big guns. But when she moves to the Hollywood years, everything becomes less fresh and more familiar. Finally, she turns to Goop: this was a story I hadn’t been paying much attention to lately, and so a genuine surprise of the book is to learn that the company founded by Paltrow in 2008 has been a much shakier business than advertised.

The difficulty is that Paltrow is a charmless subject who never rises to the level of monstrous. She’s an OK actor, a so-so businesswoman – Kim Kardashian, as Odell points out, has had much greater success with her company, Skims. The story, then, is less about how Paltrow became this figure in the culture than why on earth she was elevated in the first place. Emma Brockes
